skill.name: skill-creator
skill.description: Guide for creating Claude Code skills with correct frontmatter, folder placement, naming, Git workflow, and testing steps.
skill.summary: Create and update Claude Code skills using consistent structure and publishing steps.
skill.author: ChinchillaEnterprises

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, network, and weak-crypto patterns, but most command and crypto alerts are Markdown formatting or examples rather than executable package code. Real risk remains because the skill instructs agents to write under hidden Claude configuration folders and to use Git commands that can publish skill content to a hardcoded community repository. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious behavior was found, so publication is reasonable with a clear warning.

medium_findings.title: Hidden Claude Configuration Writes
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly directs users or agents to create and edit files under hidden Claude skill folders. This is legitimate for skill authoring, but it changes AI-agent configuration and should be reviewed before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden-folder paths are direct evidence, and the surrounding context confirms they are intended file operations. Risk is moderate because the behavior is expected for this skill type but affects agent configuration.

medium_findings.title: Shell and Git Command Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill contains command examples for mkdir, vim, git add, git commit, git pull, and git push. These commands are not executed by the package, but following them can modify repositories and publish content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands appear in Markdown examples and are semantically part of the workflow. They are not automatic execution, so the concern is user-directed operational risk rather than malware.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded Community Repository Publication Path
medium_findings.description: The skill points global skill publishing toward a specific GitHub repository and instructs pushing commits. This may be acceptable for the author, but marketplace users could unintentionally publish private skill content if they follow the workflow without checking remotes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded repository reference and push commands are explicit. There is no hidden network code, so this is a workflow and disclosure risk rather than confirmed exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many backticks as Ruby or shell execution. In context, these are inline Markdown paths, examples, and fenced code blocks, not executable code shipped with the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines are Markdown documentation, and the skill package contains only SKILL.md. This strongly supports a false-positive classification for code execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Documentation Text
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts map to words such as skill description and checklist text, not cryptographic APIs or algorithms. No evidence found of hashing, encryption, or signature verification logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are frontmatter, examples, or checklist prose. There is no executable cryptographic code in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Benign Workflow Language
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts refer to general skill-purpose and example-selection text. No evidence found of host enumeration, credential discovery, or environment probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe when to use the guide and common mistakes. They do not request system inventory or collect machine information.

dangerous_patterns.title: Manual Commands That Modify Local State
dangerous_patterns.description: Markdown examples include commands that create directories, edit files, and commit repository changes. Users should inspect paths and repository remotes before running them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are clear and intentional. They are dangerous only if copied without environment review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Publishing Workflow Includes Network Push
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow includes git push commands and a hardcoded GitHub repository reference. This can disclose skill contents if private data is committed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network action is user-directed through Git examples, not hidden code. The disclosure risk is still credible because publishing is an explicit workflow step.

content.value_statement: Skill authors need a clear process for creating discoverable Claude Code skills. This skill provides naming, structure, frontmatter, Git, and testing guidance in one workflow.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to create global skills versus project-local skills.
Defines expected skill naming rules and SKILL.md frontmatter requirements.
Shows folder layouts for global and project-local Claude Code skills.
Provides example shell and Git commands for creating and updating skills.
Includes checklists for testing and reviewing a new skill before use.
limitations: Guidance is focused on Claude Code conventions, not every AI skill platform.
It includes command examples but does not provide automated validation scripts.
It assumes a ChillSkills repository workflow that may not fit every user.
Users must review commands before running them in their own environment.

use_cases.title: Create a Personal Skill
use_cases.description: Build a reusable Claude Code skill with the expected folder name, frontmatter, and SKILL.md structure.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Skill Updates
use_cases.description: Use one checklist for updating shared skills, committing changes, and testing that activation still works.

use_cases.title: Teach Skill Authoring Rules
use_cases.description: Onboard contributors to global versus project-local placement, naming conventions, and publishing expectations.

prompt_templates.title: Begin a New Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a reusable skill.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to help me create a Claude Code skill named <name> for <task>. Ask only for details you need.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Skill Placement
prompt_templates.scenario: Decide whether a skill should be global or project-local.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to decide where my <task> skill should live. Explain the placement decision and required folder structure.

prompt_templates.title: Review Existing Skill Structure
prompt_templates.scenario: Check a current skill for naming, frontmatter, and file layout problems.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my existing skill at <path>. Identify structure issues and propose the smallest fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Skill for Sharing
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for publishing a reusable skill.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to prepare <skill-name> for sharing. Check frontmatter, naming, testing steps, and Git workflow risks before any push.

output_examples.input: Create a reusable code review skill.
output_examples.output: A concise plan that selects a global skill location, proposes a lowercase skill name, lists required frontmatter, and outlines validation steps.

output_examples.input: Should this documentation updater be project-local?
output_examples.output: A placement recommendation that compares project specificity with reusable behavior, then names the expected folder and file layout.

output_examples.input: Review my current skill before I publish it.
output_examples.output: A checklist-style review covering frontmatter, file naming, command risks, testing status, and repository publication readiness.

best_practices: Review hidden-directory and Git commands before running them.
Keep skill descriptions specific because they control activation.
Test the skill with realistic prompts before publishing it.
anti_patterns: Do not publish project-specific or secret-containing skills to a shared repository.
Do not use vague descriptions that make activation unreliable.
Do not run copied shell commands without checking paths and remotes.

faq.question: Does this skill create skills automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides instructions and command examples for creating Claude Code skills.

faq.question: Can I use it with Codex?
faq.answer: It can inform Codex workflows, but the written conventions target Claude Code skill folders.

faq.question: Why does it mention hidden folders?
faq.answer: Claude Code stores skills in hidden configuration folders, so the guide references those paths.

faq.question: Is the GitHub repository required?
faq.answer: No. The referenced ChillSkills repository is a suggested workflow from the author, not a universal requirement.

faq.question: What should I check before pushing a skill?
faq.answer: Check for secrets, private project details, correct remotes, and whether the skill should be shared.

faq.question: Does it include validation tooling?
faq.answer: No. It includes manual testing guidance, but no bundled validation script is present.
